details.body:
finding_summary: Failure reconciliation is still dead code at runtime, so failed bead tasks never update milestone lineage/controller state.
affected_files: src/contexts/automation_runtime/mod.rs, src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs, src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs
files_and_lines: src/contexts/automation_runtime/mod.rs:8,25-28 only exports the new handler; src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:1103-1105 and 1732-1734 still invoke only `try_reconcile_success`, while actual run failures go straight to `DaemonTaskService::mark_failed` at src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:1177-1188 without any failure reconciliation.
remediation_hint: add a failure-side reconciliation entry point in the daemon, parallel to `try_reconcile_success`, and invoke it before marking the daemon task failed so `BeadFailed`, retry counts, and `NeedsOperator` escalation are actually recorded.

finding_summary: Replaying a failed run after a partial milestone-state write will not repair missing journal/snapshot state.
affected_files: src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs, src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs, src/contexts/automation_runtime/success_reconciliation.rs
files_and_lines: src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:96-132 treats any existing failed lineage row as `already_recorded` and skips `record_bead_completion`; src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:2633-2684 shows that lineage is updated before snapshot/journal commit, so a crash can leave the failed task-run row durable while the `BeadFailed` event / failed counter never land; src/contexts/automation_runtime/success_reconciliation.rs:545-603 already uses `repair_task_run_with_disposition` to recover this exact class of replay.
remediation_hint: mirror the success path here: detect an already-terminal run and route through `repair_task_run_with_disposition` (or equivalent) so replay repairs missing `BeadFailed` journal/snapshot state instead of treating the run as a no-op.

finding_summary: Duplicate failure callbacks are not idempotent once a retry has started; they can clobber or reject the active retry's controller state.
affected_files: src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs, src/contexts/automation_runtime/task_service.rs, src/contexts/milestone_record/controller.rs
files_and_lines: src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:341-398 only short-circuits replays when the controller is already in the desired failed state; otherwise it always re-enters `sync_controller_task_reconciling` and transitions again. `retry_task` reuses the same daemon task id at src/contexts/automation_runtime/task_service.rs:485-515, and `sync_controller_task_reconciling`/`validate_active_context_alignment` at src/contexts/milestone_record/controller.rs:545-590 and 1469-1498 will either push a currently running retry back to `Reconciling`/`Blocked` or reject the replay if a newer task owns the bead.
remediation_hint: make the replay guard key off the reconciled run identity and/or detect a newer nonterminal attempt in lineage before mutating controller state.

finding_summary: `MAX_FAILURE_RETRIES` will not trigger for ordinary task retries, because the retry path reuses the same workflow `run_id` and the lineage layer reopens the old failed row in place. `failure_attempt_number()` treats an existing `(project_id, run_id)` failure as already recorded and reuses its original ordinal (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:320-331`), failed daemon tasks are resumed via `resume_run_with_retry_and_capture_run_id` (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:4267-4285`), and resume keeps the prior run id (`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:7029-7030`). On start, `record_task_run_start()` calls `reopen_failed_exact_attempt()` for that same run id instead of appending a new attempt row (`src/adapters/fs.rs:4546-4571`, `src/adapters/fs.rs:5014-5030`). The result is that repeated resume failures keep a single failed lineage entry, so attempt 2/3 are never observed and escalation to `NeedsOperator` never happens. remediation_hint: either persist a distinct lineage attempt for each resume of a failed run, or derive retry counts from durable failure/resume cycles rather than unique run ids.

finding_summary: Retryable failures lose the identity of the bead that is supposed to be retried. On the retryable branch, `reconcile_failure()` transitions to `Blocked` without preserving `bead_id` / `task_id` (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:383-394`, `src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:484-490`). `Blocked` is one of the controller states that explicitly clears active context (`src/contexts/milestone_record/controller.rs:1441-1447`), and blocked resumes are then treated as generic selection state that only asks whether any ready bead exists (`src/contexts/milestone_record/controller.rs:1210-1219`). `milestone next/run` also immediately re-enters bead selection whenever the controller is `Blocked` (`src/cli/milestone.rs:648-656`). That means the new "retry remains available" state is not actionable for the failed bead itself: the system either stalls as a generic blocked milestone or advances to some other ready bead, instead of preserving targeted retry state for the failed bead.

finding_summary: Failure reconciliation depends on a durable `RunFailed`/`RunStarted` pair in the project journal and aborts otherwise (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:1903-1912`, `src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:1925-1939`), but the workflow engine explicitly treats `run_failed` journal persistence as best-effort and ignores append failures (`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:6165-6177`). In that real partial-write scenario the daemon still marks the task failed, but `try_reconcile_failure()` returns `reconciliation_metadata_error` and never records the milestone-side `BeadFailed` event or retry/escalation state. remediation_hint: fall back to snapshot/interrupted-run metadata or existing lineage start data when the project journal is incomplete, instead of hard-failing the whole reconciliation path.

finding_summary: Failure reconciliation stores the daemon task ID in `controller.active_task_id`, but the controller runtime consistently interprets that field as the bead-backed project ID. The new daemon wiring passes `&task.task_id` into `reconcile_failure` (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:2047-2055`), and `transition_controller_after_failure` writes that value back into the blocked/needs-operator controller transitions (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:481-498`, `src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:545-552`). Both milestone resume paths then parse `active_task_id` with `ProjectId::new(...)` and require that project to exist (`src/cli/milestone.rs:810-819`, `src/cli/milestone.rs:1252-1264`, `src/cli/run.rs:989-1000`). After a daemon-managed failure, the controller now points at the daemon task record instead of the project, so `milestone run`/resume cannot reopen the failed bead and falls into missing/invalid-project errors instead of retrying.
affected_files:
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs
remediation_hint: Keep recording the daemon task ID in journal/outcome detail, but use `project_id` for controller transitions so blocked/retry resume continues to target the existing project.

finding_summary: The new blocked-retry resume branch requests a direct `Blocked -> Running` controller transition, but that edge is still illegal in the controller state machine. `resume_transition_request` now returns `MilestoneControllerState::Running` when a blocked retry task is already running (`src/contexts/milestone_record/controller.rs:1227-1240`), while `MilestoneControllerState::allows_transition_to` still only allows `Blocked -> Selecting|Claimed|Completed|NeedsOperator` (`src/contexts/milestone_record/controller.rs:27-55`). On any restart while a retried project is already running, `resume_controller` will therefore reject the state change as an illegal transition instead of adopting the in-flight retry.
affected_files:
- src/contexts/milestone_record/controller.rs
remediation_hint: Either route that resume case back through an allowed intermediate state such as `Claimed`, or explicitly add/support `Blocked -> Running` everywhere the journal/state validators expect legal transitions, with coverage for restart-during-retry.

finding_summary: The manual milestone retry path that this patch exposes does not actually use the new attempt-aware failure reconciliation logic. `execute_milestone_run` now treats blocked retry context as executable and resumes the existing project (`src/cli/milestone.rs:720-756`), but the terminal sync path it calls on failure still uses the old generic behavior: failed runs go through `sync_controller_task_claimed(...)` plus `record_bead_completion_with_disposition(...)` (`src/cli/run.rs:1336-1384`) rather than `reconcile_failure`. Because `execute_milestone_run` then maps any failed project snapshot straight to `MilestoneCommandStatus::NeedsOperator` (`src/cli/milestone.rs:737-756`), second/third failures triggered from `milestone run` are never evaluated against `MAX_FAILURE_RETRIES`; they bypass the new blocked-vs-needs-operator policy entirely.
affected_files:
- src/cli/milestone.rs
- src/cli/run.rs
remediation_hint: Route failed `run start`/`run resume` terminal sync through the same failure-reconciliation helper (or a shared attempt-aware helper) and derive the CLI result from the post-sync controller outcome instead of the raw failed project snapshot.

The `allows_transition_to` method in `src/contexts/milestone_record/controller.rs` (lines 52-55) is missing `(State::Blocked, State::Running)` and `(State::Blocked, State::Reconciling)` edges. The new `resume_transition_request` logic (line ~1229) for Blocked-with-retry-context generates these transitions when: (a) the retry task is already Running, or (b) the retry task already Succeeded. But `transition_from_current_locked` (line 966) enforces `allows_transition_to`, so these resume transitions would fail with a 'corrupt controller' error. Fix: add `| (State::Blocked, State::Running) | (State::Blocked, State::Reconciling)` to the match arms in `allows_transition_to`.

finding_summary: Failure reconciliation guesses the current run from the latest journal metadata when `workflow_run_id` is missing, so a retry/manual rerun can be reconciled against the wrong historical attempt.
severity: high
affected_files:
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:1913-1926
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:1997-2006
remediation_hint: Mirror the success path and require a durable task-to-run binding, or at minimum an exact active/interrupted snapshot attempt, before reconciling. Do not infer `run_id`/`started_at` from the latest journal entry.

finding_summary: When daemon-side failure reconciliation cannot determine milestone metadata, the task is marked failed but the milestone controller is left in its prior active state instead of being pushed to `NeedsOperator`.
severity: medium
affected_files:
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:1183-1201
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:1781-1799
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:1866-1931
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:2001-2008
remediation_hint: For milestone-backed tasks, convert hard reconciliation metadata failures into a controller `NeedsOperator` transition before returning the task failure, matching the existing success-reconciliation behavior.

finding_summary: Stale replay of an older failure can corrupt a newer retry that reuses the same `run_id`.
affected_files:
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:167-249
- src/adapters/fs.rs:4536-4583
- src/adapters/fs.rs:4720-4929
- src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:2633-2674
remediation_hint: Treat a row with the same `run_id` but a newer `started_at` as a stale replay and refuse to finalize it, or require an exact `(project_id, run_id, started_at)` match before updating a non-terminal row during failure reconciliation.

In `src/cli/run.rs` line 1304, `project_id.as_str()` is passed as the `task_id` parameter to `reconcile_failure_sync`. The daemon_loop call site correctly passes `task.task_id` for this parameter. While `task_id` is only used for diagnostics (error messages, tracing spans, and the `task_id=` field in `format_failure_outcome_detail`), this means CLI-path failure outcome details will contain `task_id=<project_id>` rather than a meaningful daemon task identifier. This is cosmetic since the CLI sync path has no daemon task concept, but the outcome_detail string is persisted in journal state and could confuse debugging. Consider passing a more descriptive value or documenting that task_id is project_id in CLI context.

finding_summary: Same-run-id retries can permanently lose an earlier failed attempt and undercount toward `MAX_FAILURE_RETRIES`. In `src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:167-193`, the stale-replay branch returns immediately whenever a newer attempt reused the same `run_id`. Because `src/adapters/fs.rs:4536-4583` reopens that failed attempt in place instead of preserving a separate lineage row, a partial write where the older `BeadFailed` journal append was the missing piece leaves no durable record of that earlier failure anywhere. The next failure is then counted as attempt 1 again, so escalation to `NeedsOperator` can be delayed past the configured retry limit. affected_files: [`src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs`, `src/adapters/fs.rs`]. remediation_hint: when a replay is still identifiable by `(run_id, started_at)`, repair or append the missing `BeadFailed` journal row without mutating the newer running attempt, so historical failures remain countable even when same-run retries reopen lineage in place.

finding_summary: Snapshot fallback can reconcile the wrong run when `workflow_run_id` is missing. In `src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:1998-2015`, `try_reconcile_failure()` accepts any `active_run`/`interrupted_run` from the run snapshot as the failed attempt when the daemon task lacks a durable run binding; `src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:2018-2051` then derives `failed_at` and the error summary for that run. If the snapshot is stale but the project journal already contains a newer durable `run_started`/`run_resumed` for a different run, the daemon will mark the older attempt failed and may block or escalate the bead based on the wrong execution. affected_files: [`src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs`]. remediation_hint: only use snapshot fallback when it matches the newest durable journal/lineage attempt; otherwise fail closed to `NeedsOperator`, consistent with the exact-binding checks already enforced for persisted `workflow_run_id`.

finding_summary: Missing historical `BeadFailed` events are never repaired once a newer attempt exists, so same-`run_id` retries can undercount failures and delay `NeedsOperator` escalation.
affected_files: `src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs`
file_refs: `src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:167-176`, `src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:209-231`, `src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:559-567`
why: `failure_attempt_number()` marks a stale attempt as `already_recorded` as soon as a failed lineage row exists, even if its `BeadFailed` journal event is missing. When a newer attempt exists, `reconcile_failure_sync()` returns early from the stale-replay branch before calling `repair_task_run_with_disposition()`, so the missing historical event is never backfilled. For resumed retries that reuse `run_id` and collapse older lineage rows, future retry counts then rely on the shortened journal history and can miss a prior failure.
remediation_hint: Before returning on a stale replay, explicitly repair a missing historical `BeadFailed` event for the exact `(project_id, run_id, started_at)` attempt, even when a newer attempt already exists.

finding_summary: Resumed attempts still bind failure metadata to the last `RunFailed` with the same `run_id`, so a retry whose current `run_failed` record is missing can be reconciled with the previous attempt's timestamp and error message instead of failing closed.
affected_files: `src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs`, `src/cli/run.rs`
file_refs: `src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:2119-2152`, `src/cli/run.rs:1273-1299`, `src/cli/run.rs:1493-1508`
why: both code paths look up terminal failure state by `run_id` only. Resumed attempts reuse `run_id`, so after `run_resumed` a stale older `RunFailed` still satisfies the lookup if the new attempt crashes before its own durable `RunFailed` is written. The newer attempt is then recorded with stale diagnostics, and `failed_at` can even precede the bound `started_at`.
remediation_hint: Match terminal failure events to the exact attempt boundary, not just `run_id` — for example by rejecting `RunFailed` events that predate the resolved `started_at` / latest `RunResumed`, and failing closed when no terminal event exists for the current attempt.

finding_summary: `try_reconcile_failure()` can reconcile a stale bound attempt after a newer durable attempt already exists. In `src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:1859-1864`, `persist_workflow_run_id()` intentionally never overwrites an existing binding, so later reruns/resumes can leave a task durably bound to an older run. The new failure path then derives `started_at` for that bound run by preferring any lineage row for the same `run_id` (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:1982-2042`) and never performs the newer-attempt checks that the `workflow_run_id == None` branch already does (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:2056-2093`). A stale `run_failed` from the old attempt can therefore be treated as the current failure, incrementing retry counts or escalating to `NeedsOperator` while a newer attempt is the real owner of the bead.
severity: high
affected_files:
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs
remediation_hint: Apply the same durable-attempt validation in the `Some(bound_run_id)` branch that the `None` branch already performs, and do not let a terminal lineage row outrank a newer `RunResumed`/snapshot boundary when choosing `started_at`.

finding_summary: `reconcile_failure_sync()` only suppresses stale replays when the newer retry reused the same `run_id`. The special-case guard at `src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:181-207` checks `newer_same_run_attempt_exists(...)`, but if the stale replay targets `run-1` and the active retry is `run-2`, the code falls through to `record_bead_completion_with_disposition(...)` at `src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:281-302`. In that state the lineage store has only the newer attempt open, so the replay turns into a hard reconciliation failure instead of a harmless no-op/repair. The CLI sync path already treats this situation as stale and returns without synthesizing lineage (`src/cli/run.rs:1203-1212`), so the failure handler is now less robust than the rest of the reconciliation logic.
severity: medium
affected_files:
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs
remediation_hint: Generalize the stale-replay guard to handle any newer active attempt for the bead/project, not just same-`run_id` retries, and skip or repair the historical event instead of trying to finalize against the newer open row.

finding_summary: `reconcile_failure_sync` derives an already-recorded attempt's ordinal from the insertion order of `BeadFailed` journal rows. When `repair_historical_failure_event` appends a missing older failure after later failures already exist, `failure_attempt_events` returns those rows in on-disk order and `failure_attempt_number` turns that order directly into `attempt_number`. A replay of a later failure can then be mis-numbered, which feeds the wrong value into `failure_outcome` and can downgrade a replayed third failure back to `Retryable` while also rewriting the durable `attempt=` detail incorrectly.
file_refs: src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:457-490, src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:580-614, src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:805-822
affected_files:
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs
remediation_hint: derive ordinals from a stable attempt key such as `started_at` (or keep repaired historical events chronologically ordered) before converting them into retry counts.

finding_summary: The CLI failure-sync path now calls `reconcile_failure_sync` with a synthetic task id (`cli-sync:<project>`). On any replay where the daemon already recorded the failed attempt, the `already_recorded` branch rebuilds `outcome_detail` from that synthetic id and pushes it through `repair_task_run_with_disposition`, which replaces the original daemon task id in both the lineage row and the `BeadFailed` journal payload. That makes a replay non-idempotent and destroys the real task provenance this feature is supposed to preserve.
file_refs: src/cli/run.rs:1313-1337, src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:177-179, src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:275-291, src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:2747-2824
affected_files:
- src/cli/run.rs
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs
- src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs
remediation_hint: when a failed attempt is already recorded, preserve the existing task id/outcome detail instead of replacing it with the CLI helper id, or make the CLI replay path a backfill-only repair that never overwrites prior provenance.

finding_summary: `MilestoneControllerState::Blocked` now accepts an active bead/task pair, but the transition validator still treats `Blocked` as a context-clearing state. Because `state_preserves_active_context()` excludes `Blocked` and `state_clears_active_context()` still includes it, transitions into and out of retry-blocked state no longer enforce that the same bead/project is preserved. A mismatched `Blocked -> Running` or `Reconciling -> Blocked` transition can therefore slip through without being rejected. See `src/contexts/milestone_record/controller.rs:1414-1500`.
severity: medium
affected_files:
- src/contexts/milestone_record/controller.rs
remediation_hint: Update the active-context preservation helpers (and add a regression test) so blocked retry states preserve bead/task identity the same way `Claimed`/`Running`/`Reconciling` do.

finding_summary: In the `workflow_run_id == None` path, failure reconciliation hard-requires `run.json` to still carry `active_run`/`interrupted_run` and immediately raises `reconciliation_no_run_binding` when that snapshot metadata is missing. That is too strict for crash-recovery cases where the durable project journal and milestone lineage already agree on the current failed attempt. The code even computes `durable_project_attempt_identity()` / `durable_lineage_attempt_identity()` just above, but does not use them here. See `src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:2044-2080` and `src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:2143-2180`.
severity: medium
affected_files:
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs
remediation_hint: When `workflow_run_id` is absent, fall back to the durable journal/lineage attempt identity if those sources agree, instead of requiring snapshot attempt metadata unconditionally.

finding_summary: Same-run retries can lose an earlier failed attempt and undercount retries after a partial milestone write.
affected_files:
- src/adapters/fs.rs:4536-4571
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:669-714
remediation_hint: Preserve historical failed same-run attempts when reopening a retry, or fold durable project/journal history into attempt counting before deciding between `Retryable` and `NeedsOperator`.

`reopen_failed_exact_attempt` rewrites the only failed lineage row in place when a retry resumes the same `run_id`. If the first failure crashed after updating lineage but before appending the milestone `BeadFailed` event, that failed row was the only durable evidence of attempt 1. On the next failure, `failure_attempt_number` counts only surviving `BeadFailed` events plus collapsed failed lineage rows, so the missing first attempt disappears and the reconciler can return `Retryable` instead of escalating at `MAX_FAILURE_RETRIES`. This breaks the safety gate in the exact partial-write recovery path this bead is supposed to harden.

finding_summary: Replayed historical failures with unstructured `outcome_detail` still repair the journal without a `task_id`.
affected_files:
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:199-208
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:458-465
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:623-634
- src/adapters/fs.rs:4929-4937
remediation_hint: When replaying an already-recorded failure, rebuild or augment any legacy/plain-text `outcome_detail` so it always carries `task_id=...`, `attempt=...`, and `error=...` before calling `repair_task_run_with_disposition`.

For already-recorded failures, the handler reuses any existing `outcome_detail` verbatim. If a legacy or partially-written row only has a plain summary like `stale failure`, `recorded_failure_provenance` never reconstructs the structured payload, and `update_task_run` can only backfill `task_id` from a `task_id=` prefix. The repaired `BeadFailed` event therefore still omits `task_id` even though the caller supplied one, which violates the new audit requirement for repaired failures.

finding_summary: `terminal_event_for_attempt` regressed the attempt-boundary calculation for resumed runs. In `src/contexts/project_run_record/queries.rs:129-166` it prefers the last durable `run_started`/`run_resumed` boundary and only falls back to the inferred `started_at` boundary when no durable boundary exists. That loses the fail-closed behavior still preserved by `terminal_status_for_running_attempt` in `src/contexts/project_run_record/queries.rs:200-206`. `sync_terminal_milestone_task_with_options` can now feed a newer lineage `started_at` into this helper (`src/cli/run.rs:1174-1185`, `src/cli/run.rs:1275-1317`) and still match an older `run_failed` from the same `run_id` if the resumed attempt crashed before `run_resumed` was durably written. In that window, CLI sync will reconcile the stale failure against the new retry and consume a retry / move the controller for the wrong attempt.
affected_files:
- src/contexts/project_run_record/queries.rs:129-166
- src/cli/run.rs:1174-1185
- src/cli/run.rs:1275-1317
remediation_hint: compute the lower boundary as `max(durable_boundary, inferred_boundary)` for `terminal_event_for_attempt`/`terminal_status_for_attempt`, and add a regression test for a resumed attempt with no durable `run_resumed` event.

finding_summary: retry escalation is driven by all known attempt starts, not by prior failed outcomes. `failure_attempt_number` assigns the current ordinal from `known_attempts(...)` (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:747-824`), and `known_attempts` includes every `BeadStarted` journal entry and every lineage row regardless of outcome (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:1058-1084`). That ordinal is then used directly by `failure_outcome` to decide retryable vs `NeedsOperator` (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:827-840`). A stray running/start-only attempt left behind by a crash or partial write therefore burns a retry even though no terminal `Failed` outcome was ever recorded, which contradicts the stated contract to count prior failed outcomes. A couple of orphaned starts can escalate the very first real failure directly to `NeedsOperator`.
affected_files:
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:747-824
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:827-840
- src/contexts/automation_runtime/failure_reconciliation.rs:1058-1084
remediation_hint: base the retry counter on terminal failed attempts only; use started/running artifacts only to identify the exact attempt being replayed or to repair missing failure records, not to increment the retry count by themselves.
